topic: What vitamin D deficiency does to your body long-term
headline: Vitamin D Deficiency: Your Body After 5 Years
imagePrompt: Cinematic medical illustration showing a human skeletal system with translucent overlay of muscles and organs, focusing on spine and bones with subtle areas of decreased density, soft blue-grey lighting highlighting bone structure, professional anatomical detail, realistic 3D render style with depth, no text or labels visible
caption:
Most people think vitamin D is just about bones. But after treating hundreds of patients, here's what I see happening long-term:

Your bones slowly lose density - not dramatically, but steadily. By year 5, fracture risk doubles. Your muscles actually get weaker and you might notice more falls or balance issues.

But here's what surprises people: your immune system quietly weakens. You catch every cold going around. Wound healing slows down noticeably.

The cardiovascular system? Higher blood pressure, increased heart disease risk. Your mood? Studies link chronic deficiency to persistent low mood and fatigue that no amount of coffee fixes.

The thing is, symptoms creep up so slowly that people normalize feeling "just okay." A simple blood test (25-OH vitamin D) tells you where you stand. Optimal range is 30-50 ng/mL.

Sunlight exposure (15-20 min daily) plus dietary sources help, but many need supplementation - especially if you're indoors most days.

Save this to remind yourself to get tested.
